content.content: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GERD) is defined by the Montreal consensus (2006) as a condition that develops when the reflux of stomach contents causes troublesome symptoms and/or complications. Troublesome is defined as symptoms occurring ≥2 days per week with at least moderate severity impacting quality of life. GERD is the most common upper GI disorder in the Western world, affecting approximately 20% of adults. The pathophysiology involves a failure of the anti-reflux barrier comprising the lower oesophageal sphincter (LOS), the diaphragmatic crural sling, and the angle of His (acute gastro-oesophageal angle that creates a flap valve mechanism).

LOS dysfunction is the central mechanism: a normal LOS has a resting pressure of 15–30 mmHg, length 3–4 cm, and at least 2 cm intra-abdominal position. Transient LOS relaxations (TLOSRs) — inappropriate LOS relaxation not associated with swallowing — are the predominant mechanism of reflux in patients with mild-moderate GERD and a competent LOS at rest. In severe GERD, true LOS hypotension (<6 mmHg resting) allows free reflux. Hiatal hernia (particularly Type I — sliding) displaces the gastro-oesophageal junction above the diaphragm, separating the LOS from the crural sling and disrupting the extrinsic component of the anti-reflux barrier. Bile reflux (duodenogastro-oesophageal reflux) contributes to Barrett's metaplasia and adenocarcinoma risk above and beyond acid alone.

Diagnostic evaluation: 24-hour ambulatory pH-impedance monitoring is the gold standard for quantifying acid and non-acid reflux events, correlating symptoms with reflux episodes (symptom index ≥50% is positive). The DeMeester score is a composite score derived from six pH-metry parameters (total time pH<4, upright time pH<4, supine time pH<4, total number of reflux episodes, number of episodes >5 minutes, longest reflux episode). A DeMeester score >14.72 defines pathological reflux. The Bravo pH capsule is a radio-telemetry capsule clipped to the oesophageal mucosa 6 cm above the squamo-columnar junction, providing 48–96 hour ambulatory pH data without a trans-nasal catheter — superior patient tolerability, ability to monitor off-PPI and on-PPI on alternate days, and captures day-to-day variability. Impedance monitoring detects non-acid reflux events (liquid, gas, mixed) in addition to acid, making pH-impedance superior to pH-alone for patients with persistent symptoms on PPI therapy.

content.title: DeMeester Score — Key Threshold
content.content: DeMeester score >14.72 = pathological acid reflux. This composite score incorporates six variables from 24h pH-metry (Johnson-DeMeester normative values). A score of 14.72 corresponds to the 95th percentile of asymptomatic controls. The DeMeester score is used for surgical candidacy assessment — a positive DeMeester score plus a positive symptom correlation index supports offering anti-reflux surgery.

Laparoscopic anti-reflux surgery (LARS) is indicated for: (1) confirmed GERD (positive pH-metry and/or oesophagitis) with incomplete symptom control on PPI therapy; (2) confirmed GERD with patient preference to avoid lifelong medication; (3) atypical symptoms (laryngopharyngeal reflux, chronic cough, asthma) with positive reflux monitoring and symptom correlation; (4) complications of GERD (Barrett's oesophagus with ongoing reflux, recurrent aspiration pneumonia); (5) hiatal hernia requiring repair (large symptomatic hernia, Type II/III/IV). Pre-operative evaluation must include upper GI endoscopy, high-resolution manometry (essential to assess oesophageal motility and guide choice of fundoplication), and pH monitoring.

content.content: The choice between Nissen (360°) and Toupet (270°) fundoplication is primarily driven by pre-operative oesophageal motility. In patients with ineffective oesophageal motility (IEM — defined as >50% failed peristaltic sequences or distal contractile integral <450 mmHg·cm·s on HRM), a 360° Nissen wrap may cause functional obstruction to oesophageal emptying, worsening post-operative dysphagia. The Toupet posterior partial fundoplication offers comparable reflux control with less post-operative dysphagia in patients with IEM. However, this principle has been questioned by the LOTUS randomised trial and other data suggesting that dysphagia rates do not differ significantly between Nissen and Toupet in selected populations.

content.name: LOTUS Trial — Laparoscopic Fundoplication vs PPI
content.population: 372 patients with chronic GERD, European multicentre RCT
content.intervention: Laparoscopic anti-reflux surgery (Nissen or Toupet) vs esomeprazole 20–40 mg daily for 5 years
content.result: Remission at 5 years: surgery 85% vs PPI 92% (not significant by adjusted analysis). Dysphagia and bloating more common in surgery arm. Regurgitation better controlled by surgery (5% vs 28% PPI).
content.practice_change: Surgery and PPI are both effective long-term treatments. Surgery is superior for regurgitation control. Patient values and symptom profile should guide choice — regurgitation-dominant GERD is better managed surgically.

content.name: REFLUX Trial — LARS vs PPI Long-term
content.citation: Grant AM et al. BMJ 2008;337:a2664 (5-year update: Wileman SM et al. BMJ 2010;341:c4664)
content.population: 810 patients with GERD, UK multicentre RCT
content.intervention: Laparoscopic anti-reflux surgery vs continued medical treatment (PPI/H2RA)
content.result: Surgery resulted in better quality of life and GERD symptom control at 5 years. However, 13% of surgical patients still used regular PPIs. Both treatments acceptable.
content.practice_change: LARS provides superior long-term QoL improvement for GERD. Both surgery and PPI are valid; patient preference is key determinant.

content.title: When to Choose Toupet Over Nissen
content.content: Toupet fundoplication (270° posterior wrap) is preferred in: (1) ineffective oesophageal motility (IEM) on HRM (>50% failed sequences or DCI <450 mmHg·cm·s); (2) previous oesophageal surgery; (3) GERD after Heller myotomy if Dor is not preferred. Nissen is preferred in: normal motility, regurgitation-dominant symptoms (tighter wrap), and revisional surgery where the anatomy is unfavourable for posterior dissection. Redo fundoplication requires careful manometry, pH studies, and fluoroscopy to understand the reason for failure before choosing the approach.

Hiatal hernia is defined as herniation of abdominal structures through the oesophageal hiatus of the diaphragm into the posterior mediastinum. Classification by the modified Hill/Skinner system: Type I (sliding hernia) — the gastro-oesophageal junction (GEJ) slides into the mediastinum, comprising 95% of all hiatal hernias; the GEJ remains the most cranial intrathoracic structure. Type II (true rolling/paraesophageal hernia, PEH) — the GEJ remains in its normal sub-diaphragmatic position while the gastric fundus herniates through a defect anterior or lateral to the oesophageal hiatus; rare (<5%). Type III (mixed/combined) — features of both Types I and II; the GEJ and fundus are both intrathoracic. Type IV — large PEH with other organs herniated (colon, small bowel, spleen, pancreas) into the thorax.

Management of Type I sliding hernias follows GERD management principles — the hernia does not require specific repair unless symptomatic or associated with GERD requiring surgery. Large symptomatic Type II/III/IV paraesophageal hernias require surgical repair, particularly with symptoms of obstruction, organoaxial volvulus, or risk of incarceration. Acute complications of PEH include gastric volvulus (organoaxial — along the long axis of the stomach; or mesenteroaxial — across the short axis), Borchardt's triad (epigastric pain, retching without vomiting, inability to pass nasogastric tube), and acute gastric ischaemia/necrosis.

Surgical repair of Type III/IV hernias involves: (1) reduction of hernia contents into the abdomen; (2) resection of the hernia sac (debated — associated with less recurrence in some series); (3) crural repair (posterior crural plication with non-absorbable sutures, supplemented with pledgets in large defects); (4) anti-reflux procedure (Nissen or Toupet fundoplication — almost universally added); (5) gastropexy (anterior fixation of stomach to anterior abdominal wall) in elderly frail patients without fundoplication; (6) oesophageal lengthening procedure (Collis gastroplasty) if a shortened oesophagus is encountered — a gastric tube is fashioned along the lesser curve to extend the 'neo-oesophagus'.

content.name: HIATUS Trial — Mesh vs No Mesh Hiatal Hernia Repair
content.citation: Memon MA et al. JAMA Surg 2019;154:e191340
content.population: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 12 RCTs, 1,230 patients with large hiatal hernia
content.intervention: Mesh crural reinforcement vs primary suture cruroplasty
content.result: Mesh repair: lower hernia recurrence at 12–24 months (OR 0.46). No significant difference in dysphagia or long-term complications. Biological mesh associated with lower erosion rates than synthetic.
content.practice_change: Mesh reinforcement of the hiatal repair reduces short and medium-term recurrence. Biological mesh preferred for hiatal reinforcement due to lower erosion risk. Synthetic mesh use at the hiatus carries real erosion risk and should be avoided.

content.title: Type IV Hiatal Hernia — Acute Presentation
content.content: Organoaxial gastric volvulus in a Type IV hiatal hernia presents as Borchardt's triad: severe epigastric/chest pain, violent retching without vomiting, inability to pass a nasogastric tube. This is a surgical emergency — gastric ischaemia and necrosis can develop within hours. Management: (1) urgent upper GI endoscopy (may reduce volvulus if early); (2) urgent surgical repair (laparoscopic if haemodynamically stable, open if compromised) — reduce contents, resect any necrotic stomach, crural repair, anti-reflux procedure or gastropexy.

Achalasia is a primary oesophageal motility disorder characterised by the absence of oesophageal peristalsis and failure of the lower oesophageal sphincter to relax with swallowing. The underlying pathology is progressive destruction of the inhibitory neurons (VIP-ergic and nitric oxide-ergic) in the myenteric plexus of the oesophagus and LOS, leading to an imbalance between inhibitory (relaxation) and excitatory (contraction) neural inputs — resulting in LOS hypertension and aperistalsis. The aetiology is autoimmune in most cases, with an association with HLA-DQA1 alleles; Trypanosoma cruzi infection (Chagas disease) causes secondary achalasia.

The Eckardt score is the validated clinical symptom scoring system for achalasia: dysphagia (0–3), regurgitation (0–3), retrosternal pain (0–3), weight loss (0–3). Total score 0–12. Score ≤3 = clinical success after treatment. It is used for pre-treatment assessment and post-treatment follow-up.

Absent peristalsis; IRP >15 mmHg; ≥20% pan-oesophageal pressurisation

content.content: Treatment options include: (1) Pneumatic dilation (PD) — graduated balloon dilation under fluoroscopy using Rigiflex balloons (30, 35, 40 mm); performed in series if incomplete response; success rate 70–85% short-term, declining to 50–60% at 5 years; 2–3% oesophageal perforation risk. (2) Laparoscopic Heller myotomy (LHM) + Dor (anterior) fundoplication — gold standard open/laparoscopic surgery; 6-cm myotomy on the distal oesophagus extending 2 cm onto the gastric cardia; Dor fundoplication prevents pathological reflux post-myotomy; 90% short-term success, declining to 75–85% at 5 years. (3) Peroral endoscopic myotomy (POEM) — endoscopic submucosal tunnelling to perform a myotomy of the circular muscle layer of the oesophagus and gastric cardia from the inside; highly effective for all subtypes, particularly Type III where the myotomy length can be extended along the spastic segment; 90–95% success. Key limitation: GERD develops in up to 60% post-POEM (no fundoplication possible) — requires lifelong PPI or fundoplication if severe.

content.name: ESCAPE Trial — POEM vs Heller Myotomy
content.citation: Werner YB et al. N Engl J Med 2019;381:2219-2229
content.population: 221 patients with achalasia (Types I, II, III), European multicentre RCT
content.intervention: POEM vs laparoscopic Heller myotomy + Dor fundoplication
content.result: Clinical success at 2 years: POEM 83.0% vs Heller 81.7% (non-inferiority met). POEM: higher post-procedure reflux (57% pathological pH-metry) vs Heller (20%). Complication rates similar.
content.practice_change: POEM is non-inferior to Heller myotomy for achalasia symptom control but results in significantly more pathological GERD. Heller + Dor remains preferred for patients at higher reflux risk (Barrett's, obese, LOS reflux mechanisms important).

content.title: Pseudoachalasia — Exclude Malignancy
content.content: Secondary achalasia (pseudoachalasia) mimics primary achalasia clinically and on manometry. It is caused by malignancy at the gastro-oesophageal junction (most commonly adenocarcinoma, also lymphoma, lung cancer) infiltrating the myenteric plexus. Clinical clues: age >55 years, symptom onset <6 months, rapid weight loss, absent/asymmetric fundal gas on CXR. Investigation: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, EUS (mucosal biopsy may be normal if submucosal), and repeat endoscopy with careful inspection of the gastric cardia and GEJ. NEVER attribute achalasia to a primary disorder without excluding malignancy.

Barrett's oesophagus (BO) is defined as the replacement of the normal stratified squamous epithelium of the distal oesophagus by metaplastic columnar epithelium (intestinal metaplasia with goblet cells — the British definition requires endoscopic evidence of columnar mucosa in the tubular oesophagus ≥1 cm, with or without biopsy confirmation, while the American definition additionally requires goblet cells on biopsy). BO is a complication of chronic GERD and is found in approximately 10–15% of patients undergoing endoscopy for GERD symptoms. The annual risk of progression from BO to oesophageal adenocarcinoma (OAC) is approximately 0.3–0.5% per year in non-dysplastic BO — an 11-fold relative risk compared to the general population.

The Prague C&M classification describes the endoscopic extent of BO: C = circumferential extent (cm above GEJ); M = maximal extent (cm above GEJ). For example, Prague C3M5 = 3 cm circumferential + 5 cm maximal extent (tongues/islands). This standardised reporting is essential for surveillance comparability.

content.title: Barrett's Oesophagus — Surveillance Intervals and Endoscopic Treatment
rows: Lifestyle + PPI; surveillance endoscopy with Seattle protocol biopsies (4-quadrant biopsies every 2 cm)
6–12 months after PPI optimisation (2 months high-dose PPI)
Repeat endoscopy after PPI — if persistent IND, manage as LGD
6 months to 1 year; confirmed by 2nd expert pathologist
Endoscopic eradication therapy (RFA preferred) — reduces progression to HGD/cancer by 25% (AIM-LGD trial)
EMR of any visible lesion FIRST (complete staging) + RFA (HALO) for flat HGD. If T1a or T1b sm1 = endoscopic management; T1b sm2+ = surgical resection
Post-EMR surveillance at 3, 6, 12 months, then annually
EMR + RFA for complete eradication; oesophagectomy not required
EMR for staging + risk stratification: sm1 (<500μm depth, no LVI) = endoscopic; sm2+ = oesophagectomy

content.content: Radiofrequency ablation (RFA, HALO system) uses a balloon or focal catheter delivering 10–12 J/cm² of circumferential or focal radiofrequency energy to ablate the Barrett's segment after EMR of visible lesions. Complete eradication of intestinal metaplasia (CE-IM) is achieved in 70–85% of patients after 2–3 sessions. The ASPECT trial (UK RCT) demonstrated that RFA significantly reduced the rate of progression to HGD or cancer in LGD patients. Endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR) is performed for visible lesions within Barrett's (nodules, polyps, flat lesions) before RFA — it provides accurate histological staging (critical for T1a vs T1b distinction and management) and therapeutic resection. Cap-assisted EMR or ligation-EMR (band ligation) are the standard techniques.

content.name: ASPECT Trial — RFA for Low-Grade Dysplasia in Barrett's
content.population: 136 patients with LGD in Barrett's oesophagus, European multicentre RCT
content.result: RFA: disease progression to HGD/adenocarcinoma 1.5% vs surveillance 26.5% (RR 0.06). CE-IM 92.6% RFA vs 27.9% surveillance at 3 years.
content.practice_change: RFA is the standard of care for confirmed LGD in Barrett's oesophagus — it dramatically reduces progression to cancer and achieves durable eradication of dysplastic mucosa.

content.content: Benign oesophageal strictures arise from intrinsic or extrinsic causes. Peptic strictures (most common benign stricture) occur in the distal oesophagus at the squamo-columnar junction from chronic acid exposure causing fibrosis. They are typically smooth, regular, and concentric on barium swallow. Management: high-dose PPI, endoscopic dilation (Savary-Gillard bougies or through-the-scope balloon dilators), intralesional steroid injection for recalcitrant strictures, and rarely surgical intervention. Anastomotic strictures post-oesophagectomy or post-gastrectomy occur in 5–20% of cases — managed with serial endoscopic dilations. Caustic (corrosive) strictures from acid or alkali ingestion require acute management (neutralisation, airway assessment) followed by staged dilations — surgery (colonic interposition or gastric pull-up) is reserved for strictures not amenable to dilation.

Boerhaave syndrome is spontaneous oesophageal perforation from a sudden dramatic increase in intra-oesophageal pressure against a closed cricopharyngeus — typically during violent vomiting, Valsalva, or instrumentation. The tear is almost always at the left posterolateral wall of the distal third of the oesophagus (weakest point). Mackler's triad classically describes: (1) vomiting or retching; (2) chest pain (sudden severe); (3) subcutaneous emphysema. However, the triad is present in only 14% of cases. Free mediastinal air (pneumomediastinum), left-sided pleural effusion, and contrast (water-soluble — Gastrografin first, then dilute barium if negative) swallow demonstrating extravasation are the key diagnostic features.

content.title: Pittsburgh Classification for Oesophageal Perforation Severity
rows: Age <75 years; no malignancy; cervical perforation; minimal contrast extravasation; diagnosis within 24h; no fluid in mediastinum or pleura; no tachycardia/fever
Conservative: nil by mouth, IV antibiotics, PPI, NG decompression, close monitoring; surgery if deteriorating
1 risk factor: age >75, delayed presentation (24–48h), location (thoracic), moderate mediastinal soiling, pleural fluid without empyema
Primary repair (if <24h, tissue viable) + mediastinal drainage; or T-tube drainage; oesophageal stent (SEMS) if repair not possible
≥2 risk factors, delayed >48–72h, malignancy, severe mediastinal soiling, empyema, systemic sepsis, established fistula
Oesophageal diversion (spit fistula) or stent + drainage; resection ± reconstruction in staged approach; ICU admission

content.content: Oesophageal perforation management has evolved significantly with the widespread availability of self-expanding metal stents (SEMS) and endoscopic vacuum therapy (EVT). Primary repair (within 24 hours, two-layer closure over a T-tube with pleural decortication and drainage) remains the gold standard for acute diagnosis in fit patients. Self-expanding covered metal stents seal the perforation, allow mediastinal/pleural drainage to continue externally, and can be removed endoscopically after 6–8 weeks. Endoscopic vacuum therapy (EVACs — sponge inserted endoscopically into the perforation cavity, connected to continuous negative pressure drainage) has emerged as highly effective for contained perforations and anastomotic leaks. Oesophageal exclusion (cervical oesophagostomy + gastrostomy for feeding, distal oesophagus stapled) is reserved for the most severe cases.

content.title: Mackler's Triad — Sensitivity Only 14%
content.content: Mackler's triad (vomiting + chest pain + subcutaneous emphysema) is pathognomonic of Boerhaave syndrome when present — but only present in 14% of cases. A high index of suspicion is required. Any patient presenting with chest pain and mediastinal air after vomiting requires urgent water-soluble contrast swallow or CT chest with oral contrast to exclude oesophageal perforation. Early diagnosis (within 24 hours) is the single most important prognostic factor.

Peptic ulcer disease (PUD) affects the stomach (gastric ulcer, GU) and proximal duodenum (duodenal ulcer, DU). The aetiology is disruption of the balance between mucosal protective factors (mucus-bicarbonate layer, prostaglandins E2/I2, mucosal blood flow, surface phospholipids, epithelial renewal) and aggressive factors (acid, pepsin, H. pylori virulence factors, NSAIDs). Helicobacter pylori infection is present in 70–90% of DU and 60–70% of GU. NSAIDs are the second most common cause, acting via COX-1 inhibition reducing prostaglandin synthesis (prostaglandins stimulate mucus/bicarbonate secretion and mucosal blood flow). Stress ulcers (Curling's ulcer — burns; Cushing's ulcer — head injury) occur in critically ill patients from reduced mucosal perfusion.

H. pylori eradication is mandatory for all H. pylori-positive peptic ulcers. First-line therapy (NICE 2014): standard triple therapy — PPI (standard dose BD) + clarithromycin 500 mg BD + amoxicillin 1 g BD for 7–14 days (14 days superior to 7 days). If penicillin allergy: PPI + clarithromycin + metronidazole. Second-line (failed triple therapy or clarithromycin-resistant area): bismuth-based quadruple therapy — PPI BD + bismuth subcitrate QDS + tetracycline QDS + metronidazole TDS × 14 days. Culture-guided therapy is recommended where clarithromycin resistance is >15–20% (common in Southern Europe). Confirmation of eradication by urea breath test or stool antigen test at ≥4 weeks after completing treatment is essential.

Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (ZES) is caused by a gastrinoma — a gastrin-secreting neuroendocrine tumour arising in the gastrinoma triangle (between the cystic duct, the second portion of duodenum, and the neck of pancreas) in 90% of cases. It causes acid hypersecretion, multiple peptic ulcers (often distal duodenal or jejunal — an unusual location), watery diarrhoea, and malabsorption. Diagnosis: fasting serum gastrin >1000 pg/mL is diagnostic; secretin stimulation test (paradoxical rise in gastrin >200 pg/mL after IV secretin 2 U/kg) is the most specific provocative test. Localisation: CT/MRI, endoscopic ultrasound (EUS — highest sensitivity 80–90%), and Ga-68 DOTATATE PET-CT (highly sensitive, replacing octreotide scintigraphy). 25–30% of ZES are part of MEN1 syndrome (check calcium, PTH, prolactin).

content.title: Forrest Classification — Upper GI Bleeding from Peptic Ulcer
