Endoscopic therapy (dual); high-dose PPI infusion (80 mg bolus then 8 mg/h × 72h)
Endoscopic therapy (dual); high-dose PPI; admit for 72h
Irrigate to remove clot; if underlying IIa = treat; if clear base = IIc management
High-dose oral PPI; early discharge if haemodynamically stable
Oral PPI; same-day or next-day discharge if stable

content.title: Peptic Ulcer Complications and Surgical Options
content.content: Complications of PUD requiring surgery: (1) Bleeding: failed endoscopic + IR treatment → surgery (under-running of the bleeding vessel via gastrotomy/duodenotomy; Polya gastrectomy for posterior DU bleeding from gastroduodenal artery); (2) Perforation: Graham omental patch for acute anterior DU perforation (laparoscopic preferred); (3) Obstruction: from pyloroduodenal scarring → endoscopic balloon dilatation first; surgical drainage (Heineke-Mikulicz pyloroplasty) if recurrent; (4) Malignant transformation: all gastric ulcers must have biopsies at endoscopy — rebiopsy at 6–12 weeks to confirm healing (healing does not exclude malignancy).

questions.stem: A patient with GERD and proven impaired oesophageal motility (DCI 350 mmHg·cm·s, 60% failed peristaltic sequences) requires anti-reflux surgery. Which fundoplication is most appropriate?

options.A: Nissen 360° total fundoplication — most durable anti-reflux control
options.B: Toupet 270° posterior partial fundoplication — reduced dysphagia risk with impaired motility
options.C: Dor 180° anterior partial fundoplication — always preferred to avoid posterior dissection
options.D: No fundoplication — operate only for hiatal hernia repair given poor motility

explanation.correct: B — In patients with ineffective oesophageal motility (IEM: DCI <450 mmHg·cm·s and/or >50% failed peristaltic sequences), a 360° Nissen fundoplication creates a tight wrap that may obstruct oesophageal emptying, worsening dysphagia. The 270° Toupet posterior partial wrap provides adequate acid control with less post-operative dysphagia in this setting.
explanation.wrongA: Nissen 360° is preferred in patients with normal motility. With impaired motility, the complete wrap may cause functional oesophageal obstruction and severe post-operative dysphagia.
explanation.wrongC: Dor (anterior 180°) is used primarily after Heller myotomy for achalasia — not for primary GERD. It has the least anti-reflux efficacy of the three options.
explanation.wrongD: Hiatal hernia repair without an anti-reflux procedure is appropriate only for Type II/III hernia repair in the elderly where the functional outcome of fundoplication is secondary — not for a GERD patient with impaired motility.
explanation.guideline: SAGES guidelines for anti-reflux surgery: HRM is mandatory pre-operatively; IEM is the primary indication for partial (Toupet) over total (Nissen) fundoplication.
explanation.pearl: Chicago Classification threshold for IEM: >50% failed peristaltic sequences OR DCI <450 mmHg·cm·s. In practice: DCI 350 and 60% failed sequences = clear IEM = choose Toupet.

questions.stem: A patient with breakthrough GERD symptoms despite standard-dose PPI (omeprazole 20 mg OD) is being evaluated for possible pH monitoring. What is the primary indication for pH-impedance monitoring over pH-alone monitoring in this setting?

options.A: pH-impedance is cheaper and faster than pH-alone monitoring
options.B: pH-impedance detects non-acid reflux events (liquid, gas, or mixed), which may account for persistent symptoms on PPI therapy despite adequate acid suppression
options.C: pH-impedance monitoring can be performed while the patient remains on their PPI therapy
options.D: pH-alone monitoring cannot calculate the DeMeester score and therefore misses most pathological reflux

explanation.correct: B — In patients with persistent symptoms on PPI therapy, the question is whether symptoms are caused by weakly acidic (pH 4–7), non-acid (pH >7), or bile reflux events not suppressed by PPIs. pH-alone monitoring cannot detect these events. pH-impedance simultaneously measures impedance changes (liquid/gas bolus passage) and pH, allowing detection and characterisation of ALL reflux events regardless of acidity.
explanation.wrongA: pH-impedance is more expensive and technically complex than pH-alone monitoring — not cheaper.
explanation.wrongC: Both pH and pH-impedance monitoring should ideally be performed off PPI (7 days washout) to assess the degree of acid reflux. However, for specific evaluation of symptom correlation ON therapy, monitoring can be performed on PPIs — this is a distinct indication not specific to impedance.
explanation.wrongD: Standard pH monitoring absolutely can calculate the DeMeester score — this was developed using 6-parameter pH-metry. The limitation of pH-alone is failure to detect non-acid events, not inability to calculate DeMeester score.
explanation.guideline: BSG GERD guideline (2019): pH-impedance monitoring is preferred over pH-alone when: symptoms persist despite PPI, atypical reflux symptoms, and pre/post-operative assessment.
explanation.pearl: DeMeester score threshold = 14.72. Calculated from 6 variables of 24h pH-metry. pH-impedance adds non-acid reflux detection on top of DeMeester — it does not replace it.

questions.stem: The ESCAPE trial compared POEM to laparoscopic Heller myotomy for achalasia. For which achalasia subtype does POEM offer a specific technical advantage over Heller myotomy?

options.A: Type I achalasia with megaoesophagus
options.B: Type II achalasia with pan-oesophageal pressurisation
options.C: Type III (spastic) achalasia with extensive spastic contractions involving the oesophageal body
options.D: Type II achalasia in elderly patients with poor surgical fitness

explanation.correct: C — Type III achalasia involves spastic contractions of the oesophageal body (not just the LOS) causing chest pain and dysphagia. Heller myotomy is traditionally limited to the distal oesophagus (6 cm) and gastric cardia (2 cm) — it does not adequately address the proximal spastic segment. POEM can extend the myotomy proximally to the level of the spastic contractions (sometimes 12–15 cm), targeting the underlying pathology throughout the oesophageal body. This is POEM's major technical advantage for Type III.
explanation.wrongA: Type I achalasia (megaoesophagus) responds to all treatments but may ultimately require oesophagectomy in end-stage disease. POEM does not have a specific advantage over Heller for Type I.
explanation.wrongB: Type II achalasia has the best overall response to all treatments (POEM, Heller, PD). POEM is preferred for its minimally invasive nature but does not have a specific anatomical advantage over Heller for Type II.
explanation.wrongD: Elderly patients with poor fitness may actually be better served by endoscopic dilation (PD) rather than POEM or Heller — POEM requires general anaesthesia and carries procedural risks.
explanation.trial: ESCAPE trial (Werner 2019, NEJM): POEM non-inferior to Heller overall. Type III achalasia was a pre-specified subgroup where POEM's extended myotomy is expected to outperform Heller's limited myotomy.
explanation.pearl: Type III achalasia treatment hierarchy: POEM preferred (extends myotomy along spastic body) > Heller + Dor (limited myotomy) > PD (less effective for Type III than I/II).

questions.stem: A patient with Barrett's oesophagus and confirmed high-grade dysplasia (HGD) on endoscopic biopsies using the Seattle protocol has a 1.5 cm mucosal nodule at the GEJ. What is the priority first step in management?

options.A: Immediate radiofrequency ablation (RFA) of the entire Barrett's segment
options.B: Referral for oesophagectomy — HGD with nodule represents high cancer risk
options.C: Endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR) of the nodule for histological staging before determining further treatment
options.D: Repeat surveillance endoscopy in 3 months with additional biopsies

explanation.correct: C — A visible lesion (nodule) in Barrett's HGD requires EMR BEFORE any ablative therapy. EMR provides: (1) complete histological staging — distinguishing T1a (mucosal, endoscopic management appropriate) from T1b sm2+ (submucosal invasion requiring surgery); (2) therapeutic resection of the visible lesion. RFA is then applied to the remaining flat Barrett's segment to achieve CE-IM after EMR staging.
explanation.wrongA: RFA before EMR of a visible lesion is incorrect — RFA ablates the mucosal surface and destroys histological architecture, preventing accurate staging. Performing RFA first may mask an underlying submucosal cancer.
explanation.wrongB: Oesophagectomy is not the immediate next step for HGD with a nodule. Modern endoscopic staging (EMR) stratifies patients — only T1b sm2+ or higher requires surgery. Oesophagectomy for HGD/T1a HGD when endoscopic treatment is available is an over-treatment.
explanation.wrongD: Repeat surveillance without treatment for confirmed HGD is inappropriate and would delay definitive management — HGD requires action.
explanation.guideline: BSG Barrett's guidelines (2014) and ESGE guidelines: EMR is mandatory for all visible lesions in Barrett's before ablative therapy. EMR provides histological staging critical for management decisions.
explanation.pearl: EMR always before RFA if there is a visible lesion. EMR = stage + treat. RFA = ablate flat mucosa after visible lesions removed. T1a = endoscopic; T1b sm2+ = oncology MDT discussion for surgery.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man presents acutely with severe chest pain, vomiting, and subcutaneous emphysema after a prolonged episode of retching. CXR shows pneumomediastinum and left pleural effusion. A water-soluble contrast swallow shows contrast extravasation at the distal oesophagus. CT scan shows localised mediastinal soiling but no free peritoneal air or empyema. He is haemodynamically stable and 18 hours from symptom onset. According to the Pittsburgh classification, what management approach is most appropriate?

options.A: Conservative: nil by mouth, IV antibiotics, PPI, close monitoring — no intervention
options.B: Primary oesophageal repair with mediastinal drainage via left thoracotomy or VATS
options.C: Oesophageal diversion (spit fistula) with feeding gastrostomy — too late for primary repair
options.D: Immediate oesophagectomy and gastric pull-up

explanation.correct: B — This patient is Pittsburgh Class II (18h presentation, thoracic perforation, moderate mediastinal soiling, no empyema, haemodynamically stable). Primary repair at 18 hours is still possible — the tissue remains viable and repairable within 24 hours in most cases. Left thoracotomy or VATS allows two-layer repair of the oesophageal tear plus mediastinal irrigation and drainage. An oesophageal stent is an alternative if primary repair fails or is technically not possible.
explanation.wrongA: Conservative management is appropriate for Class I (cervical or otherwise low-risk) perforations. This thoracic perforation with mediastinal soiling and pleural effusion requires active intervention.
explanation.wrongC: Oesophageal diversion (Class III management) is reserved for presentations >48–72 hours with established infection, empyema, or very unfit patients. At 18 hours in a stable patient, primary repair is the preferred option.
explanation.wrongD: Immediate oesophagectomy for benign perforation without underlying malignancy is excessive and associated with very high morbidity. It is reserved for perforation through a malignant oesophagus.
explanation.guideline: Pittsburgh classification guides management: Class I = conservative; Class II = primary repair ± stent; Class III = diversion/drainage.
explanation.trial: Systematic review (Biancari 2013): mortality for oesophageal perforation treated within 24h = 14%; after 24h = 27%. Early diagnosis and treatment is the key determinant of survival.

questions.stem: Which three components make up Mackler's triad in Boerhaave syndrome?

options.A: Haematemesis, chest pain, and fever
options.B: Vomiting, chest pain, and subcutaneous emphysema
options.C: Epigastric pain, inability to pass a nasogastric tube, and non-productive retching (Borchardt's triad)
options.D: Dysphagia, mediastinal widening on CXR, and pleural effusion

explanation.correct: B — Mackler's triad for Boerhaave syndrome consists of: (1) vomiting or violent retching; (2) sudden severe chest/epigastric pain; (3) subcutaneous emphysema (surgical emphysema in the neck/chest). This triad is pathognomonic when present — but occurs in only 14% of cases.
explanation.wrongA: Haematemesis is not part of Mackler's triad. Fever may develop later but is not part of the classic triad.
explanation.wrongC: This describes Borchardt's triad for acute gastric volvulus (in paraesophageal hernia): epigastric pain + retching without vomiting + inability to pass NG tube. Both triads must be learned.
explanation.wrongD: Mediastinal widening and pleural effusion are radiological findings supporting the diagnosis — not components of the clinical triad.
explanation.guideline: Early diagnosis within 24 hours is the most important prognostic factor in Boerhaave syndrome. Water-soluble contrast swallow remains the first-line diagnostic test after CXR.
explanation.pearl: Two triads to memorise: Mackler's (Boerhaave) = Vomiting + Chest pain + Subcutaneous emphysema. Borchardt's (Gastric volvulus/PEH) = Epigastric pain + Non-productive retching + Inability to pass NG tube.

questions.stem: What is the DeMeester score threshold that defines pathological acid reflux on 24-hour pH-metry?

options.A: DeMeester score >7.5
options.B: DeMeester score >14.72
options.C: DeMeester score >20.0
options.D: DeMeester score >25.0

explanation.correct: B — The DeMeester score threshold for pathological acid reflux is >14.72. This was established by Johnson and DeMeester (1974) as the 95th percentile of their normal asymptomatic control population. The composite score incorporates six parameters from 24-hour pH-metry: total % time pH<4, upright % time pH<4, supine % time pH<4, total reflux episodes, episodes >5 minutes, and longest reflux episode.
explanation.wrongA: 7.5 is not the established DeMeester threshold.
explanation.wrongC: 20.0 is not the threshold — scores above 14.72 are pathological, including those in the 15–20 range.
explanation.wrongD: 25.0 is too high — would miss patients with mild but pathological reflux (DeMeester 14.73–24.99) who are surgical candidates.
explanation.guideline: Johnson-DeMeester normative values: threshold 14.72 based on 95th percentile of 50 asymptomatic volunteers.
explanation.pearl: DeMeester score = composite of 6 pH-metry parameters. Threshold: 14.72. Above = pathological reflux. Used alongside symptom correlation index (>50% = positive) for surgical decision-making.

questions.stem: A Type IV hiatal hernia presents acutely with Borchardt's triad. CT scan shows the entire stomach in the chest with organoaxial rotation. What is the immediate management?

options.A: NG tube insertion in A&E and elective repair when haemodynamically stable
options.B: Upper GI endoscopy to decompress the stomach, followed by definitive surgical repair
options.C: Urgent surgical repair (laparoscopic or open): reduction of contents, assessment for ischaemia, crural repair, anti-reflux procedure or gastropexy
options.D: CT-guided percutaneous decompression of the intrathoracic stomach

explanation.correct: C — Acute gastric volvulus with Borchardt's triad is a surgical emergency. Endoscopy may temporise (reduce the volvulus) but definitive surgical repair is mandatory — ischaemia can develop rapidly. The surgical approach includes: reduction of herniated contents, resection of any necrotic stomach (partial or total gastrectomy if needed), repair of the hiatal defect, and anti-reflux procedure or gastropexy.
explanation.wrongA: NG tube cannot pass because of the volvulus (inability to pass NG tube = Borchardt's triad component). Elective repair is not appropriate — this is an emergency.
explanation.wrongB: Endoscopy may be attempted first (if available urgently) to decompress and untwist the volvulus, buying time for operative preparation. However, surgical repair cannot be delayed — the acute presentation requires emergency surgery.
explanation.wrongD: CT-guided percutaneous decompression of an intrathoracic stomach is not a recognised procedure and would not address the mechanical volvulus or the hiatal defect.
explanation.guideline: SAGES guideline on paraesophageal hernia: acute gastric volvulus = emergency surgical repair. Even in elderly frail patients, acute volvulus carries higher mortality from non-operative management than from urgent surgery.
explanation.pearl: Borchardt's triad = incarcerated/volvulised paraesophageal hernia until proven otherwise. Emergency → reduce, assess for ischaemia, repair. Mortality from untreated acute gastric volvulus approaches 100%.

questions.stem: The ESCAPE trial demonstrated that POEM results in significantly more pathological GERD than Heller myotomy. What is the main reason for this difference?

options.A: POEM divides the internal and external oesophageal muscle layers, destroying the entire sphincter complex
options.B: POEM does not allow addition of a fundoplication, whereas Heller myotomy is routinely combined with a Dor anterior fundoplication
options.C: POEM uses thermal energy which damages adjacent vagal nerve fibres and impairs oesophageal peristalsis
options.D: POEM is performed under general anaesthesia which alters LOS tone permanently

explanation.correct: B — After Heller myotomy, a Dor (anterior 180°) or Toupet (posterior 270°) fundoplication is routinely added to prevent pathological GERD from the disrupted LOS. POEM is performed entirely endoscopically through a submucosal tunnel and does not allow construction of a fundoplication. Consequently, POEM patients have a disrupted LOS with no anti-reflux mechanism — resulting in 57% pathological acid reflux (pH-metry) vs 20% after Heller + Dor in the ESCAPE trial.
explanation.wrongA: POEM divides only the circular muscle layer — the longitudinal muscle is preserved. It does not destroy the 'entire sphincter complex'.
explanation.wrongC: POEM uses submucosal dissection — the thermal energy (with haemostatic devices) is directed into the submucosal tunnel, not toward vagal nerve fibres.
explanation.wrongD: General anaesthesia does not permanently alter LOS tone.
explanation.trial: ESCAPE trial (Werner 2019): GERD rate after POEM 57% (pH-metry) vs 20% after Heller + Dor. 68% POEM patients vs 32% Heller patients on regular PPIs at 2 years.
explanation.pearl: POEM vs Heller: equivalent symptom control, but POEM = more GERD. Practical implication: POEM patients need long-term PPI and regular endoscopic surveillance for Barrett's. Prefer Heller in patients with existing Barrett's or GERD risk factors.

questions.stem: A patient with confirmed Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (ZES) has a fasting serum gastrin of 1,200 pg/mL and a positive secretin stimulation test. Cross-sectional imaging (CT/MRI) fails to localise the gastrinoma. What is the next most sensitive localisation technique?

options.A: Selective intra-arterial secretin stimulation test (SASS) with hepatic vein gastrin sampling
options.B: Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS)
options.C: Ga-68 DOTATATE PET-CT
options.D: Intra-operative portal vein sampling

explanation.correct: C — Ga-68 DOTATATE PET-CT (DOTA-octreotate coupled to Gallium-68) exploits the high density of somatostatin receptors on gastrinomas (as with most well-differentiated NETs). It has superseded octreotide scintigraphy (OctreoScan) as the most sensitive functional imaging modality for gastrinoma localisation, with sensitivity >80%. It is particularly superior for small tumours and lymph node metastases.
explanation.wrongA: Selective intra-arterial secretin stimulation (SASS) with hepatic vein sampling can regionalise gastrinoma secretion to the head/body/tail of the pancreas or duodenum, but is invasive and requires angiographic expertise — it is not a first-line localisation tool after CT/MRI failure.
explanation.wrongB: EUS has the highest sensitivity (80–90%) among structural imaging tests for pancreatic/peripancreatic gastrinomas. It is an excellent choice after CT/MRI failure. However, it is operator-dependent and less sensitive for ectopic gastrinomas.
explanation.wrongD: Intra-operative portal vein sampling is only performed at surgery — it guides intra-operative resection rather than pre-operative localisation.
explanation.guideline: ENETS consensus guidelines (2016): Ga-68 DOTATATE PET-CT is the investigation of choice for gastrinoma localisation, recommended before EUS for its superior sensitivity and whole-body assessment.
explanation.pearl: ZES localisation hierarchy: CT/MRI first → if negative → Ga-68 DOTATATE PET-CT (most sensitive whole-body) + EUS (most sensitive for small pancreatic/duodenal lesions) → consider SASS if still unlocalised.

questions.stem: On urgent upper endoscopy for haematemesis, a 2 cm duodenal ulcer with a visible non-bleeding vessel is seen (Forrest IIa). What is the correct endoscopic management?

options.A: Observe and repeat endoscopy in 24 hours — Forrest IIa has only 5% rebleeding risk
options.B: Dual endoscopic therapy (adrenaline injection + thermal contact/mechanical clip) followed by high-dose PPI infusion
options.C: Single adrenaline injection (1:10,000 solution) alone — evidence does not support additional therapy
options.D: Proceed directly to surgery — Forrest IIa has 90% rebleeding risk

explanation.correct: B — Forrest IIa (non-bleeding visible vessel) carries a rebleeding risk of 40–50%. Guidelines mandate dual endoscopic therapy: adrenaline injection alone is inferior to combined therapy; mechanical clips and/or thermal contact coagulation should be added. After endoscopic haemostasis, high-dose PPI infusion (80 mg IV bolus then 8 mg/h for 72 hours) maintains gastric pH>6, preventing clot dissolution and reducing rebleeding.
explanation.wrongA: 5% rebleeding risk applies to Forrest IIc (flat haematin spot) and III (clean base). Forrest IIa has 40–50% rebleeding risk — urgent endoscopic treatment is mandatory.
explanation.wrongC: Adrenaline injection alone achieves haemostasis but has high rebleeding rates. All major RCTs show dual therapy is superior to injection alone for high-risk lesions (Ia, Ib, IIa).
explanation.wrongD: 90% rebleeding risk applies to Forrest Ia (arterial spurting). Forrest IIa (visible vessel, non-bleeding) has 40–50% rebleeding risk — not 90%. Primary endoscopic therapy is appropriate.
explanation.guideline: NICE CG 141 (2012) and BSG guideline: dual endoscopic therapy for Forrest Ia, Ib, IIa. High-dose PPI infusion (80 mg bolus + 8 mg/h × 72h) after all Forrest Ia/Ib/IIa.
explanation.pearl: Forrest classification rebleeding risks: Ia 90% → Ib 50% → IIa 40% → IIb 20% → IIc 5% → III <5%. Dual therapy for Ia, Ib, IIa. PPI infusion for all high-risk (Ia, Ib, IIa, IIb).

questions.stem: What is the first-line eradication regimen for H. pylori infection in a patient WITHOUT penicillin allergy, in a region with <15% clarithromycin resistance?

options.A: PPI (standard dose) + metronidazole + tetracycline for 7 days
options.B: PPI (standard dose BD) + clarithromycin 500 mg BD + amoxicillin 1 g BD for 7–14 days
options.C: PPI + bismuth subcitrate + metronidazole + tetracycline for 14 days
options.D: PPI + clarithromycin + metronidazole for 7 days (penicillin-free triple therapy)

explanation.correct: B — Standard triple therapy remains first-line in regions with clarithromycin resistance <15%: PPI standard dose BD + clarithromycin 500 mg BD + amoxicillin 1 g BD for 7–14 days (14 days has superior eradication rates of 85–90% vs 75–80% for 7 days).
explanation.wrongA: PPI + metronidazole + tetracycline is a component of bismuth-based quadruple therapy — used as second-line, not first-line.
explanation.wrongC: Bismuth-based quadruple therapy (PBMT) is used as second-line after failed triple therapy, or as first-line in high clarithromycin resistance areas (>15–20%).
explanation.wrongD: PPI + clarithromycin + metronidazole is an alternative triple therapy for penicillin-allergic patients — not preferred over amoxicillin-based regimen in non-allergic patients due to higher metronidazole resistance.
explanation.guideline: NICE CG 184 (2014) and Maastricht VI consensus: triple therapy with clarithromycin/amoxicillin/PPI for 14 days is first-line in low-clarithromycin-resistance areas. Confirm eradication with urea breath test ≥4 weeks post-treatment.
explanation.pearl: Eradication confirmation: urea breath test (UBT) or stool antigen test ≥4 weeks after completing antibiotics, ≥2 weeks off PPI. Serology remains positive years after eradication — cannot be used for confirmation.

questions.stem: A Type IV hiatal hernia has been electively repaired. Two years later the patient presents with recurrent hernia on barium swallow. The HIATUS trial provides evidence regarding which technical aspect of repair?

options.A: Robotic versus laparoscopic approach to hiatal hernia repair
options.B: Nissen versus Toupet fundoplication for anti-reflux control post-repair
options.C: Mesh reinforcement of the crural repair reduces short-to-medium-term recurrence versus suture cruroplasty alone
options.D: Collis gastroplasty necessity for shortened oesophagus

explanation.correct: C — The HIATUS trial (meta-analysis of 12 RCTs, 1,230 patients) demonstrated that mesh reinforcement of the crural repair significantly reduced hernia recurrence at 12–24 months (OR 0.46) compared to primary suture cruroplasty alone. Biological mesh is preferred at the hiatus due to lower erosion risk compared to synthetic mesh.
explanation.wrongA: Robotic vs laparoscopic approaches were not compared in the HIATUS trial.
explanation.wrongB: Fundoplication type selection was not the subject of the HIATUS trial.
explanation.wrongD: Collis gastroplasty for oesophageal shortening was not studied in the HIATUS trial — this is a separate surgical technique decision made intra-operatively.
explanation.trial: HIATUS systematic review (Memon 2019, JAMA Surgery): mesh reinforcement reduces recurrence OR 0.46; biological mesh preferred; no significant difference in long-term complications.
explanation.pearl: Large PEH repair: recurrence at 1 year = 15–30% with suture alone vs 5–15% with mesh. Biological mesh preferred to avoid synthetic mesh erosion into the oesophagus — a devastating late complication.

questions.stem: A patient develops recurrent GERD symptoms 4 years after laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication. Barium swallow shows wrap herniation above the diaphragm. What are the indications for redo fundoplication in this setting?

options.A: Redo surgery is mandatory whenever a fundoplication fails — delay worsens surgical complexity
options.B: Redo fundoplication is indicated when: hernia confirmed; symptoms are predominantly reflux/regurgitation not controlled by PPI; alternative structural cause of failure identified (slip, disruption, tight wrap causing dysphagia); patient fit for laparoscopic surgery
options.C: Redo surgery is contraindicated — all failures should be managed with high-dose PPI lifelong
options.D: Immediate reoperation for any wrap herniation above the diaphragm regardless of symptoms

explanation.correct: B — Redo anti-reflux surgery is indicated when the reason for failure is identifiable (wrap herniation, slippage, disruption), symptoms are significant and not adequately controlled by optimised PPI, and the patient is fit for reoperative laparoscopic surgery. Pre-operative evaluation requires HRM, pH monitoring, endoscopy, and barium swallow to characterise the failure. Redo surgery carries higher morbidity than primary LARS (conversion rate, oesophageal injury risk) and should be performed in high-volume centres.
explanation.wrongA: Not all fundoplication failures require redo surgery — patients with mild symptoms controlled on PPI or those unfit for surgery may be managed medically.
explanation.wrongC: High-dose PPI is appropriate for medically managed failures but redo surgery provides definitive correction of the anatomical failure in suitable candidates.
explanation.wrongD: Wrap herniation above the diaphragm requires surgical correction only if symptomatic — asymptomatic anatomical recurrence may not require immediate intervention.
explanation.guideline: SAGES guideline on revisional anti-reflux surgery: full pre-operative workup mandatory; redo surgery in high-volume centres; identify reason for failure before choosing revisional procedure.
explanation.pearl: Reasons for Nissen failure: (1) Wrap herniation above diaphragm = crural defect; (2) Slipped Nissen = wrap around stomach (not GEJ); (3) Wrap disruption = aperistaltic oesophagus; (4) Tight wrap = dysphagia. Each failure mode has a different revisional approach.

questions.stem: The LOTUS trial compared laparoscopic fundoplication to esomeprazole for GERD over 5 years. Which domain was better controlled by surgery?

options.A: Heartburn and acid regurgitation overall
options.B: Regurgitation specifically — surgery was superior; heartburn was equivalent between arms
options.C: Dysphagia — surgery eliminated dysphagia better than PPI
options.D: All GERD symptoms — surgery was clearly superior at 5 years with 95% remission

explanation.correct: B — The LOTUS trial found overall GERD remission was similar between surgery (85%) and PPI (92%) at 5 years. However, in the surgical arm, regurgitation was significantly better controlled (5% vs 28% reporting troublesome regurgitation in PPI arm). Surgery was superior specifically for regurgitation; heartburn was equivalent.
explanation.wrongA: Overall heartburn control was comparable between surgery and PPI — surgery was not superior for heartburn specifically.
explanation.wrongC: Surgery was associated with more dysphagia than PPI (a side effect of the wrap). Surgery did not eliminate dysphagia — it introduced new dysphagia in some patients.
explanation.wrongD: 5-year remission: surgery 85% vs PPI 92% — not 95% for surgery, and PPI was numerically superior (though adjusted analysis showed non-significant difference).
explanation.trial: LOTUS trial (Galmiche 2011, JAMA): regurgitation: surgery 5% vs PPI 28% (p<0.0001). Overall remission: surgery 85% vs PPI 92% (NS after adjustment).
explanation.pearl: LOTUS clinical message: if the primary symptom is REGURGITATION (not heartburn) — surgery is significantly better. If heartburn-dominant — PPI is equally effective with less procedural risk. Patient symptom profile should drive the decision.

questions.stem: Barrett's oesophagus with confirmed low-grade dysplasia (LGD) is found on surveillance endoscopy with Seattle protocol biopsies. A second expert pathologist confirms LGD. What is the correct management per current guidelines?

options.A: Repeat surveillance endoscopy in 3–5 years as per non-dysplastic BO
options.B: Immediate oesophagectomy to prevent progression to adenocarcinoma
options.C: Endoscopic eradication therapy (radiofrequency ablation) with follow-up surveillance
options.D: Repeat endoscopy in 6 months without treatment to confirm persistence of LGD

explanation.correct: C — Confirmed LGD (confirmed by 2 expert pathologists) is an indication for endoscopic eradication therapy. The ASPECT trial demonstrated that RFA reduces progression from LGD to HGD or adenocarcinoma from 26.5% to 1.5% and achieves CE-IM in >90%. Current BSG and ESGE guidelines recommend RFA as standard care for confirmed LGD.
explanation.wrongA: 3–5 year surveillance is for non-dysplastic BO — not appropriate for confirmed LGD which has a 20–25% risk of progression to HGD/cancer over 5 years without treatment.
explanation.wrongB: Oesophagectomy is not appropriate for LGD — it carries 2–5% mortality and significant morbidity. Endoscopic treatment achieves equivalent cancer prevention with far lower risk.
explanation.wrongD: Repeating endoscopy in 6 months without treatment was the surveillance interval in the pre-RFA era for unconfirmed LGD. For confirmed LGD (2 expert pathologists), treatment — not further surveillance — is indicated per current guidelines.
explanation.trial: ASPECT trial (Phoa 2014, JAMA): RFA for LGD — disease progression 1.5% vs 26.5% surveillance. NNT = 4 to prevent one progression event.
explanation.pearl: LGD management depends on confirmation: single-pathologist LGD = repeat endoscopy after 2 months high-dose PPI, rebiopsy. Confirmed LGD (2 expert pathologists) = RFA. This is the current evidence-based approach.

questions.stem: The Eckardt score is used to assess achalasia symptom severity. A post-treatment score of ≤3 indicates what?

options.A: Complete cure — no further follow-up required
options.B: Clinical success — acceptable symptom control achieved by treatment
options.C: Partial response — further treatment required
options.D: Treatment failure — repeat procedure indicated within 3 months

explanation.correct: B — An Eckardt score ≤3 after treatment for achalasia defines clinical success. The Eckardt score assesses dysphagia, regurgitation, retrosternal pain, and weight loss (each 0–3, total 0–12). A post-treatment score ≤3 indicates that symptoms are at an acceptable level, though not necessarily zero. This threshold is used in all major achalasia clinical trials as the primary outcome.
explanation.wrongA: Eckardt ≤3 does not mean complete cure — it means acceptable symptom control. Long-term surveillance (repeat HRM, pH monitoring, symptom assessment) is still recommended at 1, 5, and 10 years.
explanation.wrongC: Partial response would be defined as Eckardt 4–6 (some improvement but not to the success threshold). Eckardt ≤3 is defined as clinical success, not partial response.
explanation.wrongD: Treatment failure is defined as Eckardt score >3 (persistent or recurrent symptoms at pre-treatment levels). An Eckardt score of ≤3 does NOT indicate treatment failure.
explanation.guideline: Chicago Classification for Achalasia uses Eckardt ≤3 as the standard primary outcome threshold in trials (including ESCAPE trial and multicentre achalasia registries).
explanation.pearl: Eckardt score = 4 items (dysphagia, regurgitation, chest pain, weight loss), each scored 0–3, total 0–12. Score ≤3 = clinical success. Score 4–12 = clinical failure requiring further treatment.

questions.stem: Non-dysplastic Barrett's oesophagus (Prague C2M4) is found in a 55-year-old man on surveillance endoscopy. His previous endoscopy 3 years ago also showed non-dysplastic BO. Multiple biopsies confirm no dysplasia. What is the next surveillance interval?

options.A: 12 months
options.B: 2 years
options.C: 3–5 years
options.D: 10 years

explanation.correct: C — Non-dysplastic Barrett's oesophagus requires surveillance endoscopy every 3–5 years per BSG (2014) and ESGE (2017) guidelines. The interval is based on segment length: <3 cm (C0M3) = every 5 years; ≥3 cm (C3 or more) = every 3 years. This patient (C2M4) has a maximal extent of 4 cm but circumferential of only 2 cm — the 3-year interval would apply per most guidelines.
explanation.wrongA: 12 months is the surveillance interval for indefinite for dysplasia (IND) or LGD — not non-dysplastic BO.
explanation.wrongB: 2 years is not a standard surveillance interval in either BSG or ESGE guidelines for non-dysplastic BO.
explanation.wrongD: 10 years would represent under-surveillance for BO with 0.3–0.5% annual cancer risk and is not recommended.
explanation.guideline: BSG Barrett's guidelines (2014): Non-dysplastic BO: <3 cm = 5 years; ≥3 cm = 3 years. ESGE (2017): BO <1 cm = no surveillance; 1–3 cm = 5 years; 3–10 cm = 3 years.
explanation.pearl: Surveillance interval depends on segment length (C and M classifications) and dysplasia grade. Memorise: No dysplasia = 3–5 years; IND = 6–12 months; LGD confirmed = RFA; HGD = immediate EMR + RFA.

questions.stem: A patient has a DeMeester score of 32 on pH-metry performed off PPI. The symptom correlation index is 75%. GERD-HRQL score is 22/50. High-resolution manometry shows normal motility (DCI 850 mmHg·cm·s, <20% failed sequences). Which anti-reflux procedure is most appropriate?

options.A: Toupet 270° fundoplication — all patients with GERD benefit from partial wrap
options.B: Nissen 360° fundoplication — normal motility supports a complete wrap with maximum anti-reflux effect
options.C: Dor 180° fundoplication — least invasive option
options.D: Linx magnetic sphincter augmentation — preferred when HRM shows normal IRL

explanation.correct: B — This patient has confirmed pathological GERD (DeMeester 32, well above threshold of 14.72), strong symptom correlation (75% SCI), significant QoL impairment, and NORMAL oesophageal motility (DCI 850, <20% failed sequences). Normal motility supports a 360° Nissen fundoplication, which provides the most durable anti-reflux effect. Toupet is reserved for impaired motility.
explanation.wrongA: Toupet is specifically indicated for impaired motility to reduce dysphagia risk. With normal motility, Nissen provides superior long-term acid control.
explanation.wrongC: Dor (anterior 180°) is used after Heller myotomy for achalasia, not for primary GERD. Its anti-reflux efficacy is insufficient for primary GERD treatment.
explanation.wrongD: Linx (magnetic sphincter augmentation) is an alternative for mild-moderate GERD with normal anatomy. It is not specifically indicated based on 'normal IRL' — IRL (integrated relaxation pressure) is an achalasia parameter, not a GERD parameter.
explanation.guideline: Pre-operative HRM: normal motility → Nissen 360°. Impaired motility → Toupet 270° or Dor 180°. This is fundamental to anti-reflux surgical decision-making.
explanation.pearl: The single most important manometry finding guiding fundoplication choice: peristaltic reserve (% successful sequences + DCI amplitude). Normal = Nissen. Impaired (>50% failed or DCI <450) = Toupet.

questions.stem: A patient presents with confirmed achalasia Type I (megaoesophagus, sigmoid oesophagus, Eckardt score 9). Manometry shows IRP 28 mmHg, 100% failed peristalsis, no pan-oesophageal pressurisation. What treatment approach is most appropriate?

options.A: Pneumatic dilation alone as first-line treatment — least invasive option
options.B: POEM — superior for all types including Type I megaoesophagus
options.C: Laparoscopic Heller myotomy + Dor fundoplication with or without prior oesophageal emptying; oesophagectomy if end-stage sigmoid megaoesophagus
options.D: Botulinum toxin injection into the LOS — safe and effective long-term

explanation.correct: C — Type I achalasia (particularly with sigmoid megaoesophagus) has the most advanced structural changes. For classic Type I without sigmoid deformity, Heller + Dor or POEM are appropriate. For end-stage sigmoid megaoesophagus (massively dilated, tortuous oesophagus failing all other treatments), oesophagectomy with gastric pull-up is the definitive treatment. POEM and Heller are still attempted first in sigmoid oesophagus without end-stage changes.
explanation.wrongA: Pneumatic dilation is less effective for Type I achalasia (lowest response rates among subtypes). It is less durable than surgery. It remains an option for patients unfit for surgery.
explanation.wrongB: POEM is effective for all types including Type I, but for sigmoid megaoesophagus, outcomes are less predictable and oesophagectomy should be planned if POEM/Heller fail.
explanation.wrongD: Botulinum toxin LOS injection is only a temporising measure — efficacy lasts 3–6 months, loses effect with repeated injections, and increases fibrosis making subsequent myotomy more difficult. It is not appropriate first-line for Type I achalasia.
explanation.guideline: ESGE and SAGES guidelines: Heller + Dor or POEM are equivalent first-line surgical options for Types I and II. For end-stage sigmoid megaoesophagus (Eckardt 9, failed prior treatment, massively dilated): consider oesophagectomy.
explanation.pearl: Type I achalasia treatment decision tree: (1) Heller + Dor or POEM if not sigmoid/end-stage. (2) If sigmoid megaoesophagus without end-stage features — POEM/Heller still attempted. (3) If true end-stage sigmoid megaoesophagus, failed multiple treatments — oesophagectomy with gastric pull-up.

consolidation.intro: Test your knowledge of benign oesophageal disease and oesophageal surgery with 10 clinical questions.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman presents with progressive dysphagia to solids and liquids for 18 months, weight loss of 6kg, and regurgitation of undigested food. Barium swallow shows a 'bird-beak' tapering of the distal oesophagus. Oesophageal manometry shows aperistalsis of the oesophageal body and incomplete lower oesophageal sphincter relaxation. What is the diagnosis and the treatment of choice for a fit patient?

options.A: Peptic stricture — endoscopic dilation
options.B: Achalasia — laparoscopic Heller myotomy with partial fundoplication
options.C: Achalasia — pneumatic balloon dilation
options.D: Diffuse oesophageal spasm — calcium channel blockers

explanation.correct: The clinical presentation (dysphagia to solids AND liquids, aperistalsis, incomplete LES relaxation, bird-beak appearance) is classic for achalasia. For a fit patient, laparoscopic Heller myotomy (LHM) with partial fundoplication (Dor or Toupet) provides durable symptom relief in 85–95% of patients at 5 years. It is superior to single pneumatic dilation for long-term durability.

wrong.A: Peptic stricture causes dysphagia to solids (not liquids early), occurs in GORD context, and does not show aperistalsis on manometry. The bird-beak appearance with manometric findings is diagnostic of achalasia.
wrong.C: Pneumatic dilation achieves 80–85% short-term success but has a 50% recurrence rate at 5 years and requires repeat procedures. LHM is preferred for fit patients. The POETSS trial showed POEM (per-oral endoscopic myotomy) to be equivalent to LHM.
wrong.D: Diffuse oesophageal spasm presents with chest pain and dysphagia, with simultaneous high-amplitude contractions on manometry. Incomplete LES relaxation and aperistalsis are not DES features — this is achalasia.

explanation.guideline: ISDE/ACG Achalasia Guidelines 2020 — LHM with fundoplication as preferred surgical treatment; POEM as equivalent endoscopic option
explanation.trial: POETSS trial (Ponds FA, Gastroenterology 2019): POEM vs pneumatic dilation — POEM: 92% success at 2 years vs 54% for single dilation; equivalent to LHM at 5 years.
explanation.pearl: Achalasia subtypes (Chicago Classification v4.0): Type I (classic, no pressurisation), Type II (panesophageal pressurisation — best surgical outcomes 95%), Type III (spastic, premature contractions — POEM preferred over LHM as longer myotomy needed). Always exclude pseudoachalasia (malignancy at gastro-oesophageal junction) with CT/EUS.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a 15-year history of GORD, currently on PPI, undergoes upper GI endoscopy showing Barrett's oesophagus (2cm segment, Prague classification C1M3). Four-quadrant biopsies confirm intestinal metaplasia with no dysplasia. What is the recommended surveillance interval?

options.A: Repeat endoscopy in 6 weeks to confirm diagnosis
options.B: Annual endoscopy with four-quadrant biopsies
options.C: Endoscopy every 2–3 years for non-dysplastic Barrett's ≥3cm (long-segment)
options.D: No further endoscopy needed — non-dysplastic Barrett's requires no surveillance

explanation.correct: BSG 2023 and ASGE 2022 guidelines for non-dysplastic Barrett's oesophagus: long-segment (≥3cm) → surveillance endoscopy every 2–3 years. Short-segment (<3cm) → every 3–5 years. This patient has Prague C1M3 (circumferential 1cm, maximal extent 3cm), placing him in the long-segment category.

wrong.A: Re-biopsy at 6 weeks is not recommended after confirmed intestinal metaplasia on multiple four-quadrant biopsies. The diagnosis is established.
wrong.B: Annual endoscopy is appropriate for low-grade dysplasia (LGD) after confirmation and treatment decision. Non-dysplastic Barrett's does not warrant annual surveillance.
wrong.D: Non-dysplastic Barrett's carries 0.3% per year risk of progression to oesophageal adenocarcinoma. Surveillance is recommended, particularly for long-segment disease, as part of agreed BSG/ASGE guidelines.

explanation.guideline: BSG Barrett's Oesophagus Guidelines 2023 — surveillance intervals: non-dysplastic short-segment 3-5y; long-segment 2-3y
explanation.trial: Hvid-Jensen F et al., NEJM 2011: population-based Barrett's surveillance — annual cancer incidence 0.12%; cancer risk increases with segment length; surveillance justified for ≥3cm.
explanation.pearl: Prague classification: C = circumferential extent (cm above GOJ); M = maximum extent. C1M3 = circumferential 1cm, maximum 3cm. Key surveillance thresholds: non-dysplastic: 2-3y (long); LGD confirmed: ablation (RFA) or 6-monthly surveillance; HGD: endoscopic ablation mandatory; T1a cancer: endoscopic resection (EMR/ESD).

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden severe chest pain and dysphagia after forceful vomiting. CXR shows a left pleural effusion and mediastinal air. CT confirms oesophageal perforation with left pleural contamination. He is 8 hours from the event and haemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Conservative management — nil by mouth, IV antibiotics, and chest drain
options.B: Primary surgical repair of the perforation with tissue flap reinforcement
options.C: Endoscopic covered stent insertion to seal the perforation
options.D: Emergency oesophagectomy

explanation.correct: Boerhaave syndrome (spontaneous oesophageal perforation from forceful vomiting) requires urgent management. At 8 hours with pleural contamination, primary surgical repair (left thoracotomy, two-layer closure, reinforced with pleural/intercostal muscle flap or diaphragm patch) is the treatment of choice. Success rates are 80–90% when repaired within 24 hours. Left thoracotomy also allows pleural debridement and drain placement.

wrong.A: Conservative management (nil by mouth, antibiotics, chest drain) is appropriate only for very small, contained perforations in a clinically well patient with no mediastinal contamination. Established pleural contamination requires surgical or endoscopic intervention.
wrong.C: Covered oesophageal stents can seal perforations and are a viable option for perforations detected late (>24h) or in patients unfit for surgery. At 8 hours with a stable patient, primary repair achieves better outcomes.
wrong.D: Emergency oesophagectomy is not indicated for Boerhaave syndrome. It is only considered if the perforation is at a site of malignancy or if primary repair fails with extensive necrosis.

explanation.guideline: WSES Oesophageal Perforation Guidelines 2019 — primary repair within 24h for Boerhaave; stent for late presentation or high surgical risk
explanation.trial: Biancari F et al., Ann Thorac Surg 2014: oesophageal perforation meta-analysis — primary repair: mortality 18%; endoscopic stent: 19%; conservative: 11% (but only for contained/small perforations). Surgery preferred for pleural contamination.
explanation.pearl: Mackler's triad (Boerhaave): vomiting → chest pain → subcutaneous emphysema. CXR: left pleural effusion, mediastinal air. Confirm with water-soluble swallow or CT with oral contrast. Gastrografin first (aspiration risk of barium); if negative but strong suspicion, dilute barium or CT.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with progressive dysphagia to solids, a sensation of food sticking in the throat, gurgling sounds, and halitosis. She occasionally regurgitates undigested food eaten hours earlier. Barium swallow shows a posterior pharyngeal diverticulum at the level of Killian's dehiscence. What is the management?

options.A: Endoscopic cricopharyngeal myotomy and septotomy (stapled or laser)
options.B: Open transcervical diverticulectomy with cricopharyngeal myotomy
options.C: Conservative management — dietary modification
options.D: PEG tube insertion for long-term nutrition

explanation.correct: Zenker's diverticulum (posterior pharyngeal diverticulum at Killian's dehiscence) is managed endoscopically in most symptomatic patients. Flexible or rigid endoscopic stapling/laser septotomy divides the cricopharyngeus muscle (the bar between the diverticulum and the oesophagus), creating a common cavity. This is less invasive than open surgery, with equivalent symptom relief (85–90%) and faster recovery.

wrong.B: Open transcervical diverticulectomy with cricopharyngeal myotomy achieves equivalent success (85–90%) but carries higher complication rates (recurrent laryngeal nerve injury, fistula). It is reserved for very large (>5cm) diverticula, endoscopic failure, or contraindications to endoscopy.
wrong.C: Dietary modification does not treat Zenker's diverticulum. The diverticulum will continue to enlarge and risk aspiration. Symptomatic diverticula require procedural treatment.
wrong.D: PEG tube insertion is a last resort for patients with severe dysphagia who cannot be fed orally and are unfit for any procedure. It does not treat the diverticulum.

explanation.guideline: ESGE Guidelines for Upper GI Endoscopy 2022 — endoscopic septotomy preferred for Zenker's diverticulum
explanation.trial: Verdonck J et al., Laryngoscope 2020: systematic review — endoscopic vs open Zenker's — equivalent efficacy (85-90%); endoscopic: shorter hospital stay, fewer complications; preferred approach.
explanation.pearl: Zenker's diverticulum arises at Killian's dehiscence (between thyropharyngeus and cricopharyngeus muscles of inferior pharyngeal constrictor) — a weak posterior point. The diverticulum is not in the true oesophagus but in the pharynx. Cricopharyngeal hypertonia is the underlying cause — myotomy addresses this.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man has a 3.5cm type II (rolling/paraesophageal) hiatus hernia on CT, with the gastric fundus herniating into the chest alongside the gastro-oesophageal junction. He is asymptomatic. What is the recommended management?

options.A: Watchful waiting — repair only if symptoms develop
options.B: Elective laparoscopic hiatus hernia repair because all paraesophageal hernias require surgical repair
options.C: Long-term PPI therapy
options.D: Elective laparoscopic repair given risk of acute gastric volvulus and strangulation

explanation.correct: The management of asymptomatic paraesophageal (type II) hernia is controversial. The SAGES 2013 guidelines and contemporary data do not support universal elective repair for asymptomatic paraesophageal hernias. The annual risk of acute presentation (gastric volvulus, strangulation) is 1.1–1.2%. In older/comorbid patients, elective repair carries 1-2% mortality that may equal the natural history risk. Watchful waiting with patient education about acute symptoms is appropriate for asymptomatic patients.

wrong.B: The dogma of 'all PEH require repair' has been challenged by observational data showing low acute complication rates. Asymptomatic PEH in elderly or high-risk patients can be safely observed.
wrong.C: PPIs treat GORD symptoms but do not prevent mechanical complications of paraesophageal herniation (volvulus, strangulation, Cameron's erosions). Long-term PPI is not the definitive management.
wrong.D: While acute gastric volvulus is a risk, the absolute annual risk is low (1.1%) and elective repair carries morbidity. This logic applies to symptomatic PEH — elective repair is indicated for symptoms, not simply for risk prevention in asymptomatic patients.

explanation.guideline: SAGES Guidelines for Hiatal Hernia Repair 2013 — elective repair for symptomatic PEH; watchful waiting acceptable for asymptomatic PEH
explanation.trial: Stylopoulos N et al., Ann Surg 2002: decision analysis for asymptomatic PEH — watchful waiting preferred up to age 65; annual complication risk 1.1%; elective repair: 1.4% mortality at age 65.
explanation.pearl: Symptomatic PEH (GORD, anaemia from Cameron ulcers, post-prandial pain, dysphagia) warrants elective laparoscopic repair. Type III (mixed) and Type IV (with other organs) are more complex — combined Nissen/Toupet fundoplication with crural repair and mesh reinforcement.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with heartburn and regurgitation refractory to twice-daily PPI for 3 months. 24-hour pH impedance study confirms pathological acid reflux (DeMeester score 48) despite PPIs. High-resolution manometry shows normal peristalsis and LES resting pressure 8 mmHg (low). He declines ongoing medication. What is the surgical treatment of choice?

options.A: Laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication (360° wrap)
options.B: Laparoscopic Toupet fundoplication (270° posterior wrap)
options.C: Magnetic sphincter augmentation (LINX device)
options.D: Laparoscopic anterior fundoplication (180°)

explanation.correct: For GORD with pathological acid reflux confirmed on pH impedance, normal oesophageal peristalsis, and low LES pressure, laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication (360° wrap) is the gold standard surgical treatment. It achieves pH normalisation in >90% and freedom from PPIs in 85–90% at 10 years. Normal peristalsis (essential prerequisite) means the 360° wrap can be tolerated without significant dysphagia.

wrong.B: Toupet (270°) is preferred when peristalsis is weak (amplitude <30 mmHg) to reduce dysphagia risk. With normal peristalsis, Nissen provides superior acid control.
wrong.C: LINX magnetic sphincter augmentation is an effective alternative for mild-to-moderate GORD with intact peristalsis, achieving 85% freedom from PPIs. It is particularly suitable for patients with concerns about fundoplication complications. However, Nissen remains the gold standard for durability.
wrong.D: Anterior 180° fundoplication has lower dysphagia rates but also lower anti-reflux efficacy (75% freedom from PPIs vs 85–90% for Nissen). Not the first-line surgical option in a fit patient with good peristalsis.

explanation.guideline: ISDE GORD Surgical Guidelines 2022 — laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication as gold standard for pathological GORD with normal peristalsis
explanation.trial: LOTUS trial (Lundell, Gut 2011): laparoscopic Nissen vs esomeprazole — GORD control at 5 years: 85% vs 93%; both effective; surgery preferred in patients who desire medication-free life.
explanation.pearl: Pre-operative requirements for Nissen: (1) confirmed pathological GORD on pH/impedance; (2) HRM showing normal peristalsis (amplitude >30 mmHg); (3) no Barrett's HGD/cancer; (4) hiatus hernia repair if present. Weak peristalsis → Toupet. Failed previous fundoplication → LINX or redo Nissen at expert centre.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with achalasia (Type II, confirmed on HRM) undergoes POEM (per-oral endoscopic myotomy). At 3 months follow-up she reports excellent dysphagia relief (Eckardt score 1) but has new daily heartburn. Upper GI endoscopy shows LA grade B oesophagitis. What is the most appropriate management of this complication?

options.A: Redo POEM — oesophagitis indicates incomplete myotomy
options.B: Long-term PPI therapy — GORD is an expected consequence of POEM without fundoplication
options.C: Laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication
options.D: No treatment — LA grade B oesophagitis resolves spontaneously

explanation.correct: GORD is the primary disadvantage of POEM compared to laparoscopic Heller myotomy (LHM). POEM does not include fundoplication, resulting in pathological GORD in 40–50% of patients. This is managed with long-term PPI therapy in most cases. Some patients require magnetic sphincter augmentation (LINX) or fundoplication if PPIs fail. The dysphagia relief and GORD trade-off is the key POEM vs LHM comparison point.

wrong.A: Redo POEM is not indicated for post-POEM GORD. GORD after POEM results from absence of anti-reflux mechanism, not incomplete myotomy. Incomplete myotomy causes persistent dysphagia, not oesophagitis.
wrong.C: Laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication is a surgical escalation reserved for PPI-refractory post-POEM GORD. PPI therapy is first-line and effective in most patients.
wrong.D: LA grade B oesophagitis (breaks in mucosa <5mm) requires treatment to prevent progression to Barrett's and oesophageal stricture. PPIs are safe, effective, and inexpensive.

explanation.guideline: ISDE Achalasia Guidelines 2020 — post-POEM GORD: PPI long-term; 40-50% incidence; annual Barrett's surveillance
explanation.trial: POETSS trial: GORD after POEM in 49% vs 10% after LHM; PPI effectively managed in 90%; annual surveillance endoscopy recommended after POEM.
explanation.pearl: POEM vs LHM comparison: POEM — superior for Type III achalasia (longer myotomy possible), minimal surgical invasiveness; LHM — adds fundoplication reducing GORD. Post-POEM surveillance for Barrett's is essential given elevated GORD rates. POEM GORD incidence: 40-50% at pH monitoring, but symptomatic in only 30%.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman has Barrett's oesophagus (C3M5) with confirmed low-grade dysplasia (LGD) on two sets of biopsies 3 months apart, reported by an expert GI pathologist. She is on twice-daily PPI. What is the preferred treatment?

options.A: Oesophagectomy
options.B: Radiofrequency ablation (RFA) of the entire Barrett's segment
options.C: Surveillance endoscopy every 6 months without treatment
options.D: Continue PPI and repeat endoscopy in 2 years

explanation.correct: Confirmed LGD in Barrett's oesophagus warrants endoscopic ablation (radiofrequency ablation — RFA) of the entire Barrett's segment. The AIM Dysplasia trial showed RFA for LGD reduces 5-year progression to HGD/cancer from 26.5% to 1.5%. BSG 2023 and ASGE 2022 guidelines recommend RFA for confirmed LGD as first-line treatment.

wrong.A: Oesophagectomy carries 2–5% mortality and significant morbidity. It is reserved for T1b or higher oesophageal cancer, or HGD/T1a cancer not amenable to endoscopic resection. LGD is not an indication for oesophagectomy.
wrong.C: Surveillance every 6 months without treatment is not optimal for confirmed LGD. The AIM Dysplasia trial demonstrates significantly higher progression rates without ablation.
wrong.D: Continuing PPI and repeating endoscopy in 2 years is the recommendation for non-dysplastic Barrett's, not confirmed LGD. LGD requires ablation.

explanation.guideline: BSG Barrett's Guidelines 2023 — RFA for confirmed low-grade dysplasia
explanation.trial: AIM Dysplasia trial (Shaheen NJ, NEJM 2009): RFA for LGD — 91% complete eradication of dysplasia vs 23% sham; 5-year cancer incidence 1.5% vs 9.5%; NNT to prevent one cancer: 12.
explanation.pearl: Barrett's endoscopic treatment principles: (1) EMR/ESD for any visible lesion first (staging and therapy); (2) RFA for flat dysplasia ablation; (3) surveillance after eradication (3mo × 2, then annual). Eradication of intestinal metaplasia (complete eradication of IM — CEIM) is the treatment endpoint.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman underwent laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication 6 months ago. She now presents with severe dysphagia to liquids and solids, weight loss 4kg, and is unable to belch. Barium swallow shows near-complete obstruction at the level of the wrap. What is the most likely complication and initial management?

options.A: Recurrent GORD — increase PPI dose
options.B: Tight wrap causing dysphagia — endoscopic pneumatic dilation
options.C: Oesophageal cancer — urgent CT staging
options.D: Wrap herniation — laparoscopic redo fundoplication

explanation.correct: Post-Nissen dysphagia with near-complete obstruction at the wrap level 6 months post-operatively is a tight wrap. Initial management is endoscopic pneumatic balloon dilation — this stretches the wrap, providing immediate relief in 70–80% of cases. If repeated dilation fails, laparoscopic revision (conversion to Toupet or takedown) is indicated.

wrong.A: Recurrent GORD presents with heartburn and regurgitation, not dysphagia. The barium swallow shows obstruction at the wrap site, not GORD recurrence.
wrong.C: Oesophageal cancer must be considered in new dysphagia, but the context of recent Nissen fundoplication and obstruction at the wrap level makes tight wrap the diagnosis. Upper GI endoscopy would exclude malignancy.
wrong.D: Wrap herniation (intraoperative slippage of the wrap around the stomach body) presents differently on barium swallow with the wrap above the diaphragm. Redo surgery is not first-line for tight wrap — dilation first.

explanation.guideline: SAGES Fundoplication Complication Management 2022 — pneumatic dilation for post-fundoplication dysphagia
explanation.trial: Khajanchee YS et al., Arch Surg 2002: post-fundoplication dysphagia management — 70% resolved with 1-2 dilations; persistent dysphagia (30%): redo laparoscopic surgery (Toupet or takedown).
explanation.pearl: Post-Nissen complications: (1) tight wrap (dysphagia, gas-bloat, inability to belch) → dilation then revision; (2) slipped wrap (GORD recurrence + dysphagia) → redo; (3) recurrent GORD (wrap disruption) → redo Nissen or LINX; (4) gas-bloat syndrome → reassurance, simethicone, dietary modification.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man presents with progressive dysphagia over 6 months and 8kg weight loss. Oesophagogastroscopy shows a mid-oesophageal mucosal irregularity. Biopsies confirm squamous cell carcinoma. CT/PET shows no distant metastases. EUS shows T2 N1 disease. What is the staging and recommended treatment?

options.A: T2 N1 M0 — primary surgery (oesophagectomy) without neoadjuvant treatment
options.B: T2 N1 M0 — definitive chemoradiotherapy (dCRT) without surgery
options.C: T2 N1 M0 — neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy followed by transthoracic oesophagectomy (CROSS protocol)
options.D: T2 N1 M0 — endoscopic mucosal resection

explanation.correct: T2 N1 M0 oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma is locally advanced, stage IIB/IIIA. The CROSS trial demonstrated that neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy (carboplatin + paclitaxel × 5 weeks + 41.4 Gy radiotherapy) followed by oesophagectomy significantly improves R0 resection rates and 5-year OS (47% vs 34% surgery alone) for both adenocarcinoma and SCC. This is the standard of care for resectable locally advanced oesophageal cancer.

wrong.A: Primary surgery without neoadjuvant therapy for T2 N1 disease results in inferior R0 resection rates and 5-year survival. CROSS trial established neoadjuvant CRT as standard.
wrong.B: Definitive CRT (without surgery) achieves 30–40% long-term disease control for SCC, less than trimodality therapy. It is reserved for patients unfit for surgery or with cervical oesophageal location.
wrong.D: EMR is limited to T1a (mucosal) disease. T2 involves the muscularis propria — EMR cannot achieve R0 resection and has no role in T2 disease.

explanation.guideline: NCCN Oesophageal Cancer 2024 — neoadjuvant CRT + surgery (CROSS) for resectable T2+ oesophageal cancer
explanation.trial: CROSS trial (van Hagen, NEJM 2012): neoadjuvant CRT + surgery vs surgery alone — R0 resection 92% vs 69%; 5-yr OS 47% vs 34%; pCR 29% (SCC 49%, adenocarcinoma 23%).
explanation.pearl: After neoadjuvant CRT, allow 6–8 weeks before oesophagectomy (allows tumour response assessment and patient recovery). Ivor Lewis (right thoracotomy + laparotomy) for mid/distal tumours. McKeown (right thoracotomy + laparotomy + cervical anastomosis) for upper/mid tumours. MIRO trial: minimally invasive oesophagectomy reduces pulmonary complications.
